content: Athena: Odd numbers are those integers that cannot be divided evenly by two—they leave a remainder of one. Even numbers divide cleanly by two with no remainder. When you train, confirm each new number by asking, 'Does 2 divide this number exactly?' before trusting the label.
Learner: I will always check the last digit; if it is 0,2,4,6,8 then the integer is even, otherwise odd. Athena also asked me to say 'remainders matter' when I label examples.

Athena: Prime numbers greater than one are divisible only by one and themselves. You can test a prime by checking divisibility up to its square root. Record that algorithm so the model can reproduce it during fast inference.

Scribe: I write the rule down: start at two, divide, stop when the divisor squared exceeds the number; if no divisors appear, the number is prime. Athene wants to teach this until the model answers 'prime or composite' without hesitation.
